text:
Standing Barbell Overhead Press (OHP) — Setup and Starting Position

Stance: Roughly hip-width, feet parallel or slightly toed out. This is a standing press — the entire body is the structure transmitting force, so a stable stance is critical.

Bar rack position: The bar rests across the front deltoids and upper chest, in the groove of the anterior shoulder. The elbows are in front of the bar (not pointing straight out to the sides). Grip just outside shoulder-width. The wrists are stacked over the elbows — neutral, not dorsiflexed.

Core and glute tension: Before pressing, squeeze the glutes and brace the core. This prevents the classic lower back hyperextension compensation that develops when pressing overhead without trunk stability. Think: 'squeeze a coin between your glutes.'

Head path: As the bar passes the face (forehead level), move the head back slightly to clear the bar's path, then immediately 'push the head through' as the bar clears the top — the final position has the head through the window created by the arms, ears in line with biceps.

Lockout: Bar directly overhead, stacked over the mid-foot and over the hips. Arms fully locked. Shoulders shrugged slightly upward (active overhead position — do not disengage the traps at lockout as this dumps load onto the passive shoulder structures).

Descent: Reverse the path — bar comes back to the front deltoid shelf. Use controlled eccentric (2 seconds) to build shoulder stability.

Standing Barbell Overhead Press — Muscles, Errors, and Programming

Primary movers: Anterior and lateral deltoids (primary), triceps brachii (elbow extension at lockout), upper trapezius and serratus anterior (scapular upward rotation — critical for pain-free overhead pressing).

Secondary: Posterior deltoid (stabilizer), rotator cuff (dynamic stabilizer throughout), core musculature (isometric anti-extension).

Common errors:
1. Lower back hyperextension — the most common error and source of lower back pain with OHP. Indicates inadequate core bracing or insufficient thoracic extension mobility. Fix: squeeze glutes hard, brace core before each rep; if it persists, reduce load.
2. Bar path too far in front — pressing the bar in a forward arc rather than vertical increases the moment arm dramatically. Fix: keep bar over mid-foot.
3. Head not moving through at lockout — the bar ends up in front of the head rather than overhead, creating an inefficient lever. Fix: consciously push the head through the window.
4. Elbow flare (elbows pointing out to the sides at rack) — puts the shoulder in external impingement position. Fix: elbows slightly in front of the bar at the start.
5. Pressing on toes — indicates the bar is drifting forward. Fix: feel the floor through the whole foot, especially the heels.

Programming: Press responds well to wave loading (5/3/1, 3×5, etc.) with regular form checks. It is typically limited by shoulder mobility rather than absolute strength early in training — address overhead mobility before aggressive loading.
